title: Failure of the Quench Action Formalism for Mott Insulator Initial States
abstract: The quench action formalism relies on the assumption that the overlap between a generic initial state $\left|Ψ_{0}\right\rangle $ and an eigenstate of an integrable model - defined through the rapidities $\left|k_{1},...k_{N}\right\rangle $ - can be written as: \begin{equation} \left\langle k_{1},...k_{N}\midΨ_{0}\right\rangle =\exp\left(-S_{Ψ_{0}}\left(ρ\left(k\right)\right)\right),\label{eq:Exponential} \end{equation} where $ρ\left(k\right)$ is the quasiparticle density of the state $\left|k_{1},...k_{N}\right\rangle $ and $S_{Ψ_{0}}$ is some smooth function of $ρ\left(k\right)$ that depends on $Ψ_{0}$. In particular the quench action formalism assumes the overlap depends smoothly on the quasiparticle density $ρ\left(k\right)$. In this work, by explicit counter example, we show that this is not the case. We consider the quench between a Mott insulator and a Lieb Liniger gas. We show that the overlap between the ground state of the Mott insulator and arbitrary eigenstates of the Lieb Liniger gas has a highly singular behavior and no expression like Eq. (1) applies. We do so within the Tonks Girardeau limit of the Lieb Liniger gas and to leading order in the $1/c$ expansion for the overlap (with $c$ being the coupling constant of the Lieb Liniger gas). In the Appendix we show similar results for overlaps in the XXZ model with crystal states.
summary: This theoretical paper proves that the Quench Action Formalism fails to accurately describe quantum system evolution after a sudden change (quench) into an integrable model. Using explicit calculations on systems like Mott insulators quenched to Lieb-Liniger gases, the authors show that the true overlaps are highly singular, contradicting the formalism's assumption of smooth dependence.
main_problem: The paper challenges the validity of the Quench Action Formalism, a theoretical tool used to predict quantum system relaxation after a quench into an integrable model.
main_result: By explicit counterexamples involving Mott insulator quenches to Lieb-Liniger gas and XXZ models, the authors demonstrate that the overlap between initial states and eigenstates exhibits highly singular behavior, violating the smooth functional dependence assumed by the formalism.
method: The analysis involves calculating quantum overlaps using bosonic creation/annihilation operators in specific limits (e.g., Tonks-Girardeau limit) and comparing these results against the predicted exponential form of the quench action.
model_or_system: The study examines quenches between Mott insulators and Lieb-Liniger gases, as well as overlaps within the XXZ model with crystal states. The underlying physics involves integrable quantum many-body systems.
key_observables: The primary observable is the overlap $\langle k_{1},...k_{N} | \Psi_{0} angle$ between the initial state and the eigenstates of the final Hamiltonian.
important_parameters: Key parameters include the coupling constant $c$ (for Lieb-Liniger gas) and anisotropy $\Delta$ (for XXZ model), analyzed in specific limits like leading order in $1/c$.
main_assumptions: The paper critiques the core assumption of the Quench Action Formalism: that the overlap must depend smoothly on the quasiparticle density $ho(\mathbf{k})$.
figures_summary: Not specified by notes, but calculations involve comparing derived overlap expressions (e.g., Eq. 29 vs Eq. 6) to show discrepancies.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the formalism and then presents explicit counterexamples using two distinct physical systems: Mott insulator $	o$ Lieb-Liniger gas, followed by an appendix detailing similar failures in the XXZ model with crystal states.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for theorists studying non-equilibrium dynamics in quantum many-body systems, as it invalidates a widely used simplifying assumption (the quench action) for predicting thermalization or relaxation pathways.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
